---
title: "LRP77: Period-resolved dose-response — intervention dose -> word reading (W)"

date: last-modified
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss Z"

format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
    number-sections: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-links:
      - repo
---

::: {.callout-warning title="Warning: Preliminary model output"}

This model output is preliminary and likely to change as the model evolves and further data are received.

:::

```{python}
# | echo: false
import json
from pathlib import Path

import arviz as az
import pandas as pd

_here = Path(".")
with open(_here / "config.json") as f:
    config = json.load(f)
trace = az.from_netcdf(_here / "trace.nc")
```

## Overview

LRP77 is the gated [#104](https://github.com/dseinternational/language-reading-predictors/issues/104)
Phase-2 follow-up to the period-resolved gradient-boosting diagnostic
. Phase 1 found the
structure in the near-noise word-reading gain models sits on the **dose /
intervention-status axis**, with a weak positive dose signal that concentrates in
the on-treatment periods. This model quantifies that one signal with honest
uncertainty.

**Estimand.** How word-reading *conditional change* relates to the per-period
intervention **dose** (`attend`, sessions attended that period), with partial
pooling across the three periods, and whether that dose–gain slope **varies by
period**. The outcome is the Beta-Binomial post-count of $W$ conditional on its
own baseline logit ($N = 79$) — conditional change, **not** a raw change score
(Lord's paradox / regression to the mean). All three phase transitions
(t1$\to$t2, t2$\to$t3, t3$\to$t4) are stacked with phase-specific intercepts; the
dose enters standardised, so the slope is the outcome-logit change per 1 SD of
per-period dose:

$$
\eta = \alpha + \alpha_{\text{phase}[p]}
     + \beta_G\,G
     + \gamma_{\text{own}}\,\text{logit}(W_{\text{pre}})
     + \gamma_A\,z(\text{age})
     + u_{\text{child}}
     + \beta_{\text{dose}[p]}\,z(\text{attend})
     + \gamma_{\text{stage}}\,z(\text{attend\_cumul})
$$

with $\beta_{\text{dose}[p]} = \mu_{\text{dose}} + \sigma_{\text{dose}}\,z_p$
(partial-pooled period slopes).

## Adjustment set (locked DAG)

The focal edge is $\text{dose} \to \text{outcome}$. Sessions is the locked DAG's
`IS` node; this is the ID-3 **observational** dose-response (adjust $\{IG, A\}$,
never condition on `IS` itself). Dose has one parent, `intervention`, so the
back-door set is $\{G, W_{\text{pre}}, A\}$ — but for distinct reasons:

- **$G$ — the sole confounder.** Conditioning on the arm blocks the only back-door
  $\text{dose} \leftarrow \text{intervention} \to \text{outcome}$. **Coding:
  $G = 2 - \text{group}$, so $G = 1$ is the immediate-intervention arm and $G = 0$
  the waitlist control; positive effects = intervention benefit** (the `#117` sign
  convention).
- **$W_{\text{pre}}$ — not a dose confounder.** The autoregression / RTM control
  that makes this *conditional change*; parameterisation, not back-door blocking.
- **age — not a dose confounder.** v5 has $\text{age} \to \text{outcome}$ but no
  $\text{age} \to \text{dose}$, so age is a precision / maturation covariate only.

**Cumulative dose** (`attend_cumul`, prior sessions) enters as a control so a
dose-stage effect is not misread as a period effect (it is 0 for everyone in
period 1).

**Sample.** All gain rows are kept, including the waitlist controls' period-1
`attend == 0` rows — the **zero-dose anchor** that identifies the slope.

### The load-bearing assumption: no `ability -> dose`

v5 deliberately omits $g \to \text{dose}$. But Frank's 2012 caveat — "the children
least able to learn tended to show the poorest attendance" — is exactly that
edge. **Dose is not randomised** (only `intervention` is), so randomisation does
not rescue this. If $g \to \text{dose}$ holds, $g$ is a common cause of dose and
outcome and $\{G\}$ no longer identifies the dose effect. Two safeguards: the
subject random intercept $u_{\text{child}}$ absorbs *stable* child differences,
and the ability-adjusted sensitivity fit **LRP77a** conditions on the
baseline-skill cluster (letter-sounds, expressive vocabulary, blending). If the
dose slope survives there, the assumption is defensible; if it collapses, the
dose signal is substantially ability-confounded.

### Causal warrant and expectations

This is **adjusted association**, not "dose drives gains" — only the period-1 ITT
(now `lrpitt10`) is randomised. Phase-1 best strata reached only $R^2$ 0.1–0.3;
the deliverable is a calibrated dose slope with credible intervals, not a strong
predictor. Do not condition on anything downstream of dose.

![Model diagram](model_graph.svg){#fig-model .lightbox fig-align="left"}

## Shared priors

![alpha](prior_alpha.svg){width=40%}
![tau / beta_G](prior_tau.svg){width=40%}
![gamma_own](prior_gamma_own.svg){width=40%}
![gamma_cross](prior_gamma_cross.svg){width=40%}
![kappa](prior_kappa.svg){width=40%}

The dose mean $\mu_{\text{dose}}$ uses the unit-scale `beta_mech` prior
$\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$; the between-period slope SD $\sigma_{\text{dose}}$ uses
`HalfNormal(0.5)` (`priors.sigma_dose_phase_prior`).

## Sampling diagnostics

```{python}
# | echo: false
diag = pd.read_csv(_here / "diagnostics.csv", index_col=0)
diag
```

![Trace](trace_plot.png){.lightbox}
![Energy](energy_plot.png){.lightbox}

## Dose slope (the estimand)

```{python}
# | echo: false
dose = pd.read_csv(_here / "dose_slope_summary.csv")
dose
```

`dose_overall` ($\mu_{\text{dose}}$) is the pooled slope; `dose_period1..3` are
the partial-pooled per-period slopes; `sigma_dose_between_period` is their spread.
All are on the **logit scale per 1 SD of per-period dose**; `p_pos` is
$P(\text{slope} > 0)$. A positive slope means more sessions in a period predict a
larger conditional gain. Read the per-period slopes against
`sigma_dose_between_period`: small $\sigma_{\text{dose}}$ with similar period
slopes means the dose–gain relationship does **not** vary materially by period
(the period slopes shrink toward the pooled effect). Whether period variation
improves predictive fit is settled by the LRP77 vs **LRP77base** PSIS-LOO
comparison.

## Posterior predictive

![Posterior predictive check](posterior_predictive_check.png){.lightbox}

## LOO-PSIS

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | output: asis
print("```\n" + open(_here / "loo.txt").read() + "\n```")
```

## Configuration

```{python}
# | echo: false
config
```
